Hannah's prayer at the opening of this chapter reveals something startling about God that the rest of the chapter will underscore: He is not bound by human circumstance or earthly power. She sings of a God who reverses everything we assume is permanent. The mighty lose their strength; the starving are filled; the barren woman bears children. She has just placed her own son in God's service—the answer to her desperate prayer—and her joy is not in possessing him but in recognizing that her God is utterly sovereign over all outcomes. This is not sentimental thanksgiving; it is a hard-won conviction born from years of humiliation and shame. Yet even as Hannah celebrates this reversal, the chapter immediately pivots to show us how rare such faith actually is in Israel.

The sons of Eli reveal the ugliness beneath religious office. They treat God's sacrifices as their personal meal ticket, bullying worshippers and taking the best meat before it is even properly offered to the LORD. What makes their sin so grave is not merely greed but contempt—they are treating the sacred offerings with deliberate indifference toward God Himself. Eli knows what his sons are doing and rebukes them, but his rebuke carries no force. He is a man who honored his sons above God, and the text flatly states that they refused to listen because the LORD intended to put them to death. There is something terrifying in that statement: sometimes God hardens hearts not as an arbitrary punishment but as a judgment on those already bent toward wickedness. Eli's weakness as a father and his failure to act decisively open the door for deeper corruption.

Against this backdrop of religious decay, Samuel grows. The contrast is deliberate. The boy wears a simple linen ephod—the garment of priestly service—and each year his mother visits with a robe she has made for him. He grows in stature and in favor with the LORD and with man. There is no dramatic sign, no miracle in these verses, just the quiet reality of a young person remaining faithful in a corrupt place. Meanwhile, God speaks judgment through a man of God directly to Eli. The word is devastating: the priestly line Eli comes from will be cut off, no member of his household will reach old age, and they will come to poverty, begging for scraps. But the promise follows the judgment—God will raise up a faithful priest. The reversal Hannah sang about is coming, but it will first require the removal of those who despise the LORD's offering.

What must we see here? Institutional faithfulness and personal piety are not the same thing. A man can hold an important position in God's work and still be complicit in its corruption through passivity. Eli failed to truly restrain his sons; he heard their wickedness but did not act with the weight his authority demanded. Meanwhile, a boy serving faithfully in obscurity is being prepared for something larger. The chapter ends with God's promise: the faithful one will do whatever is in His heart and mind, and walk before His anointed forever. Samuel does not yet know what lies ahead, but God is already at work, building a house through faithfulness and removing houses built on contempt for Him.
